1: Job's ninth speech begins without counter-accusation. He is no longer trying to refute Eliphaz; he is searching for God.
2: Gam-hayyôm mārî mĕrî: 'Even today my complaint is bitter/rebellious' — mārî from mārar (to be bitter) or mārāh (to rebel): the ambiguity is intentional. Job's speech is simultaneously lament-bitterness and a kind of rebellion against his situation. Yādî kāḇĕḏāh ʿal-ʾanḥātî: 'the burden laid on me is heavier than my groaning can express' — kāḇēḏ (heavy, serious): the weight of his affliction exceeds his capacity to articulate it.
3: Mî-yittēn yāḏaʿtî: 'If only I knew!' — the wish-formula expressing impossible longing (cf. Job 6:8; Job 11:5). ʾEmṣāʾĕhû (find him): the dominant verb of this chapter — the hidden God who cannot be located. ʿAd-tĕḵûnātô: 'to the place where he dwells/his establishment' — tĕḵûnāh (dwelling-place, throne-setting): the divine throne-room Job longs to reach.
4: ʾEʿĕrĕḵāh lĕpānāyw mišpāṭ: 'I would lay my case before him in full order' — ʿāraḵ (to arrange, set in order): the legal preparation of a case, the same term as the altar-arrangement of sacrifice. Job would present a formally ordered lawsuit. Ûpî ʾĕmallēʾ tôḵāḥôt: 'fill my mouth with the strongest arguments' — tôḵāḥat (argument, proof, reproof): the formal counter-evidence.
5: ʾēḏĕʿāh millîm yaʿănēnî: 'I would know what he would say in reply' — Job's primary desire is not vindication but comprehension: to understand God's response. ʾāḇîn mah-yōmar lî: 'and understand every word' — the listening dimension: Job wants to hear and understand, not merely prevail.
6: Hĕḇĕrōb-kōaḥ yārîḇ ʿimmî: 'Would he use his vast power to overwhelm me in argument?' — rîḇ (to contend, argue at law): the legal dispute term. Job answers his own question: lōʾ ʾak-hûʾ yāśîm bî — 'No, he would give me a hearing.' The conviction that God would hear him in person is the bedrock of Job's faith.
7: Šām yāšār nôkāḥ ʿimmô: 'There an upright man could argue his case before him' — yāšār (upright, straight, honest): Job's self-designation. ʾăpallĕṭāh lāneṣaḥ miššōpĕṭî: 'I would receive final acquittal from my judge' — pālaṭ (to escape, be delivered) + lāneṣaḥ (forever/finally): the permanent acquittal that Job is certain the direct encounter would produce.
8: Hēn qeḏem ʾehĕlōk wĕʾênennû: 'I go east and he is not there' — the four-direction search for God. Qeḏem/ʾāḥôr (east/west) + śĕmôʾl/yāmîn (left/right — i.e., north/south): the exhaustive compass search. God's absolute hiddenness in the face of Job's suffering is the experiential problem the book wrestles with.
9: Śemôʾl bĕʿośĕhû wĕlōʾ-ʾāḥāz: 'he works on the left and I cannot seize him' — ʾāḥaz (to grasp, take hold of): Job cannot grab hold of the God who is acting (note: God is working — bĕʿăśōtô — even when hidden). Yaʿaṭōp yāmîn wĕlōʾ ʾerʾeh: 'he conceals himself on the right and I cannot see him' — the active divine hiddenness: ʿāṭāp (to cover oneself, conceal) — God wrapping himself in hiddenness.
10: Kî-yāḏaʿ derek ʿimmādî: 'But he knows the path I walk' — the pivot: Job cannot find God, but God knows Job. The asymmetry is the ground of faith. Bĕḥānānî kazzāhāḇ ʾēṣēʾ: 'when he has tried/tested me I will come out as gold' — bāḥan (to test, examine metal): the metallurgical refinement metaphor. Suffering as the smelting process that purifies rather than destroys.
11: Bĕʾăšurô ʾāḥăzāh raglî: 'My foot has stayed on his track' — the path-metaphor for covenant faithfulness. Darkô šāmartî wĕlōʾ ʾāṭ: 'I have kept his way without turning off' — nāṭāh (to turn aside): the standard warning against apostasy. Job claims unbroken path-fidelity.
12: Miṣwat śĕpātāyw wĕlōʾ ʾāmîš: 'I have not departed from his commands' — mûš (to depart, move away): the standing-order term (cf. Joshua 1:8: 'let not this Book of Torah depart from your mouth'). ʾImrê pîw ṣāpantî mēḥuqqî: 'I have valued the words of his mouth more than my daily bread' — ḥuqqî (my statute/allotment/daily portion): Job prioritizes divine speech over physical sustenance, prefiguring Matthew 4:4.
13: Wĕhûʾ bĕʾeḥāḏ ûmî yĕšîḇennû: 'But he is resolute/one in purpose, and who can turn him?' — bĕʾeḥāḏ (in one thing, resolute): God's absolute singularity of purpose, which cannot be reversed. Napšô ʾiwwĕtāh wayyaʿaś: 'what his soul desires, that he does' — the divine will as both unconstrained and unfathomable. This is the God Job cannot argue around.
14: Yĕšallēm ḥuqqî wĕkāhēnnāh: 'He will carry out what he has ordained for me' — ḥōq (statute, prescribed portion/decree): the divine allocation for Job is fixed and will be fulfilled. Wĕrabbôt kāhēnnāh ʿimmô: 'and there is much more still with him' — the divine purpose for Job exceeds what has been revealed; more is coming.
15: ʿal-kēn mippānāyw ʾebbāhēl: 'That is why I am shaken before his face' — bāhal (to be dismayed, terrified, agitated): the theophanic dread. Job's faith and fear coexist: he wants to find God and yet dreads the encounter. ʾĂḇîn wĕʾepḥad mimmennû: 'when I understand, I am afraid of him' — comprehension increases rather than decreases the dread.
16: Wĕʾēl hērak libbî: 'God has made my heart soft/weak' — rāḵaḵ (to soften, melt): the heart losing its firmness. Wĕšadday hĕḇahălānî: 'the Almighty has shaken me' — bāhal (to terrify, agitate) in Piel: intense, repeated terrifying. The very God Job seeks is the one who dismantles his composure.
17: Kî lōʾ-niṣmattî mippĕnê-ḥōšeḵ: 'Yet I am not silenced/cut off by the darkness' — ṣāmat (to cut off, destroy): Job refuses to be silenced by the surrounding darkness. The verse is difficult; the MKT renders it as Job's defiant persistence: despite everything, he does not stop. Ûmippānay kissāh-ʾōpel: 'deep darkness covers my face' — the conditions are as bad as possible, yet Job continues to speak and seek.
